procedures.intent: To extract total protein from intestinal tissue samples and quantify protein concentration for Western blot analysis.

procedures.name: Western Blot for Tight Junction Proteins
procedures.intent: To quantify the relative expression levels of claudin-1 and occludin in intestinal tissue samples.

overall_assumptions: Availability of appropriate animal housing facilities and ethical approval.
Access to a spectrofluorometer for FITC-dextran measurement and Western blot imaging system.
Trained personnel for animal handling, oral gavage, blood collection, and lab procedures.
Standard lab equipment and reagents are available.
L. rhamnosus GG stock and vehicle are prepared and stored correctly.
overall_controls: Vehicle control group (oral administration of diluent without L. rhamnosus GG).
Positive control for intestinal permeability (e.g., LPS injection or DSS administration, if relevant and ethical).
Standard chow diet and water ad libitum for all groups.

procedures.name: Animal Acclimation and Group Assignment
procedures.intent: To acclimate mice to the vivarium environment and randomly assign them to experimental and control groups.

steps.title: Receive and Unpack Mice
steps.body_md: Upon arrival, carefully unpack the C57BL/6 mice from their shipping containers. Visually inspect all mice for signs of stress, injury, or disease (e.g., lethargy, ruffled fur, wounds). Record any observations. Transfer mice to clean, standard caging.
steps.notes: Ensure vivarium staff are aware of the arrival time and have prepared the necessary cages and supplies.
steps.anticipated_outcome: All mice are safely transferred to initial housing, appearing healthy.
troubleshooting: If signs of illness or injury are observed: Segregate affected mice immediately and consult a veterinarian. Document all incidents.
If fewer mice than expected are received: Contact the supplier and vivarium management immediately.

steps.title: Initial Housing Setup
steps.body_md: House the C57BL/6 mice (age 8-10 weeks) under standard vivarium conditions. Ensure a 12-hour light/12-hour dark cycle (e.g., lights on 07:00, lights off 19:00), ambient temperature of 20-24°C, and humidity of 40-60%. Provide ad libitum access to standard chow diet and filtered water.
steps.notes: Record cage IDs, number of mice per cage (initially), and date of housing.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice are housed in appropriate, controlled conditions.

steps.title: Acclimation Period
steps.body_md: Allow mice to acclimate to the vivarium environment for a minimum of 7 days following arrival. During this period, observe mice daily for health, activity, and feeding/drinking behavior. Minimize disturbances to reduce stress.
todo_for_researcher: Specify exact start and end dates for acclimation.
steps.notes: Acclimation helps reduce stress variability between animals prior to experimental interventions.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice appear healthy, active, and have adapted to the new environment, showing no signs of stress or illness.
troubleshooting: If mice show prolonged signs of stress or illness: Extend the acclimation period, re-evaluate environmental conditions, or consult with vivarium veterinarian. Do not proceed to experimental procedures until all mice are healthy and acclimated.

steps.title: Random Group Assignment
steps.body_md: After the acclimation period, randomly assign the C57BL/6 mice to experimental groups: 'L. rhamnosus GG Supplementation' and 'Vehicle Control'. Ensure a minimum of 8 mice per group (n>=8). Use a computer-generated randomization method (e.g., random number generator) to assign individual mice to groups, ensuring an even distribution of initial body weights if possible, but strict randomization is prioritized.
todo_for_researcher: Specify the exact randomization method to be used (e.g., Python script, online calculator).
steps.notes: Record the individual mouse ID and its assigned group. Consider cage effects; ideally, distribute mice from original cages across various experimental groups.
steps.anticipated_outcome: All mice are assigned to either the experimental or control group, with an equal number of mice per group (or as close as possible for odd numbers).
troubleshooting: If randomization results in significant weight disparities between groups: Re-run randomization or stratify by weight if an equal number of cages for each condition is not feasible, though strict randomization is preferred. Document the decision.

steps.title: Individual Housing and Identification
steps.body_md: Individually house mice within their assigned experimental groups to prevent cross-contamination of oral supplements. Assign unique identifiers (e.g., ear tags, microchips, tail marks) to each mouse. Update records to include group assignment and individual identifier for each mouse.
equipment_needed: Ear tag applicator and tags OR Microchip reader and transponders OR Animal markers
todo_for_researcher: Confirm preferred method of individual mouse identification (e.g., ear tag, microchip, temporary tail marking).
steps.notes: Individual housing ensures accurate dosing and avoids potential social interactions influencing permeability or tight junction protein expression.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Each mouse is individually housed and clearly identifiable with its assigned group.
troubleshooting: If a mouse loses its identification: Immediately re-identify the mouse, or if identification is impossible, remove the mouse from the study and document. Ensure backup identification methods are in place for safety.

equipment: Ear tag applicator and tags (or microchip reader/transponders or animal markers)

success_criteria.what: All mice received are healthy and have successfully acclimated.
success_criteria.how_measured: Daily visual inspection for clinical signs of stress or illness throughout the acclimation period.
success_criteria.threshold: Absence of clinical signs (e.g., ruffled fur, hunched posture, decreased activity) in >95% of mice.

success_criteria.what: Mice are randomly assigned to experimental groups.
success_criteria.how_measured: Review of randomization records and group assignments.
success_criteria.threshold: All mice are assigned to one of the two groups (L. rhamnosus GG or Vehicle Control) with n>=8 per group, based on a documented randomization method.

success_criteria.what: Each mouse is individually identifiable and housed.
success_criteria.how_measured: Verification of unique identification for each mouse and confirmation of individual housing.
success_criteria.threshold: 100% of mice have a unique, legible identifier and are housed singly within their assigned group.

procedures.name: Daily Oral Supplementation
procedures.intent: To administer L. rhamnosus GG or vehicle control to respective mouse groups daily for 4 weeks.

steps.title: Prepare L. rhamnosus GG (LrGG) Oral Suspension
steps.body_md: Prepare a suspension of Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LrGG) at the specified concentration in filter-sterilized normal saline. The concentration should be sufficient to deliver the target CFU/dose in a small, administrable volume (e.g., 200 µL). Invert gently to mix, do not vortex aggressively.
todo_for_researcher: Determine the exact CFU/mL required based on desired dose and administration volume (e.g., if target is 1x10^9 CFU/mouse/day in 200 uL, then prepare 5x10^9 CFU/mL stock).
steps.notes: LrGG should be obtained as a lyophilized powder or frozen stock. A fresh suspension should be prepared daily to ensure viability and consistent dosing. Ensure aseptic technique during preparation to prevent contamination.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A homogeneous, milky-white suspension with no visible clumps.
troubleshooting: If suspension is clumpy: Ensure proper resuspension technique; allow lyophilized powder to rehydrate for a few minutes before gentle mixing.
If concentration is inconsistent: Verify counting method (e.g., plate counts) and preparation accuracy.

steps.title: Prepare Vehicle Control Oral Solution
steps.body_md: Prepare filter-sterilized normal saline (0.9% NaCl) to be used as the vehicle control. This solution will be orally administered to the control group in the same volume as the LrGG suspension.
steps.notes: Use the same lot of saline for both experimental and control preparations if possible to minimize variability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile saline solution.

steps.title: Restrain Mice for Oral Gavage
steps.body_md: Gently habituate mice to handling for at least 3-5 days before the start of supplementation. For daily gavage, carefully remove one mouse from its cage. Hold the mouse firmly by the scruff of its neck, ensuring its head is slightly elevated and its body is straight. This extends the neck and aligns the esophagus for easier gavage. Support the body with the other hand.
steps.notes: Proper restraint is crucial for safe and effective gavage. Practice on non-experimental mice or with a non-gavage procedure first if inexperienced. Minimize stress to the animals.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mouse is securely and comfortably restrained, ready for gavage.
troubleshooting: If mouse struggles excessively: Release, allow the mouse to calm, and reattempt. Ensure proper scruffing technique to avoid airway compression.
If mouse bites: Use bite guards or wear thicker gloves, ensure adequate scruffing to prevent head movement.

steps.title: Administer Oral Gavage (LrGG or Vehicle)
steps.body_md: Use a sterile, blunt-ended gavage needle or flexible gavage catheter of appropriate size (e.g., 20-gauge or 22-gauge for adult mice). Slowly and steadily insert the gavage needle into the mouse's mouth, guiding it along the palate and down the esophagus. It is crucial to ensure the needle enters the esophagus and not the trachea. A common sign of correct placement is the mouse's ability to swallow. Once the needle is properly positioned, slowly dispense 200 µL of the assigned solution (LrGG suspension for the study group, normal saline for the control group) over 5-10 seconds. Remove the needle gently.
todo_for_researcher: Ensure gavage needle size is appropriate for C57BL/6 mice (e.g., 20GA or 22GA, 38mm length, ball-tipped).
steps.notes: The source protocol administered 5 times/week. However, the hypothesis states 'daily oral supplementation'. This procedure adapts to daily administration. Listen for breathing sounds; if gurgling or wheezing occurs, the needle may be in the trachea. Do not proceed with injection if tracheal placement is suspected.
steps.anticipated_outcome: All mice in their respective groups receive their assigned daily oral dose.
troubleshooting: If fluid enters the trachea (mouse chokes, turns blue): Immediately stop procedure, humanely euthanize the animal, and note incidence. Review gavage technique.
If mouse expels fluid: Confirm proper gavage technique, ensure slow administration. Re-gavage if a significant amount is expelled and animal's condition allows.

steps.title: Return Mouse to Cage and Monitor
steps.body_md: After successful oral gavage, gently return the mouse to its home cage. Briefly observe the mouse for any signs of immediate distress (e.g., labored breathing, lethargy, ruffling of fur). Ensure it returns to normal activity within a few minutes.
steps.notes: Careful monitoring helps ensure animal welfare and identify any issues arising from the gavage procedure.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mouse appears healthy and returns to normal activity within minutes of gavage.
troubleshooting: If mouse exhibits prolonged distress: Humanely euthanize if severe; if mild, monitor closely for the next few hours and consult with veterinary staff.

steps.title: Repeat Daily for 4 Weeks
steps.body_md: Repeat steps 3-5 daily for 4 consecutive weeks for both the LrGG supplementation group and the vehicle control group. Ensure each mouse receives its assigned treatment consistently at approximately the same time each day.
steps.notes: Maintain a detailed record of daily gavages for each mouse, including any missed doses or adverse reactions.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice receive consistent daily dosing over the 4-week study period.
troubleshooting: If a dose is missed: Record the missed dose. Do not double-dose the next day. Continue with the regular schedule.
If daily schedule is difficult to maintain: Administer within a consistent window (e.g., +/- 1 hour from target time).

deviations_from_source.from_source: Source protocol 9400 (steps 600932, 600933) specifies oral administration '5 times/week for 2 weeks'.
deviations_from_source.to_adapted: This procedure specifies 'daily oral supplementation for 4 weeks'.
deviations_from_source.reason: The overall experiment hypothesis explicitly states 'Daily oral supplementation for 4 weeks' with L. rhamnosus GG. This is a direct adaptation to meet the experimental design.

deviations_from_source.from_source: Source protocol 9400 (step 600934) used a specific Lactobacillus mixture (SL 16) comprising multiple strains including L. rhamnosus.
deviations_from_source.to_adapted: This procedure specifies direct administration of 'Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LrGG)' as a single strain.
deviations_from_source.reason: The overall experiment hypothesis specifically targets 'Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG' as the intervention, not a mixture. This is a direct adaptation to meet the experimental design.

success_criteria.what: Daily oral administration of LrGG or vehicle control
success_criteria.how_measured: Observation during gavage; daily entries in experimental log; absence of gavage-related injury.
success_criteria.threshold: All mice in LrGG group receive LrGG, all mice in control group receive vehicle; 100% dosing frequency or documented exceptions with rationale; no more than 5% gavage-related mortality/morbidity over the 4-week period.

success_criteria.what: Animal health and welfare during supplementation period
success_criteria.how_measured: Daily visual inspection; body weight monitoring (weekly).
success_criteria.threshold: Mice maintain body weight (no more than 10% loss from baseline); no evident signs of persistent distress, poor coat quality, or abnormal behavior.

procedures.name: Intestinal Permeability Assay (FITC-dextran)
procedures.intent: To assess intestinal permeability by measuring systemic FITC-dextran levels after oral gavage.

steps.title: Prepare FITC-Dextran Stock Solution
steps.body_md: Prepare a 100 mg/mL stock solution of FITC-dextran (4 kDa) by dissolving 1 gram of FITC-dextran in 10 mL of sterile PBS. Mix thoroughly by vortexing until completely dissolved. Filter sterilize the solution using a 0.22 µm syringe filter to remove any particulate matter and ensure sterility.
steps.notes: Prepare this stock solution fresh on the day of experiments, or aliquot and store at -20°C for up to one month. Thaw on ice if using frozen stock.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, yellowish-green solution free of particulates.

steps.title: Prepare Working Solution of FITC-Dextran
steps.body_md: Dilute the 100 mg/mL FITC-dextran stock solution with sterile PBS to achieve a working concentration of 50 mg/mL. For example, if preparing 5 mL of working solution, combine 2.5 mL of stock solution with 2.5 mL of sterile PBS. Mix gently.
steps.notes: The final gavaged dose will be 60 mg/100g body weight, assuming a gavage volume of 0.12 mL/10g body weight.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, yellowish-green solution at the desired working concentration.

steps.title: Fast Mice Prior to Gavage
steps.body_md: Remove food from animal cages 4 hours prior to the scheduled gavage time. Provide free access to water during the fasting period.
steps.notes: Fasting ensures consistent gastric emptying and absorption of FITC-dextran.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice appear normal but unfed.

steps.title: Administer FITC-Dextran by Oral Gavage
steps.body_md: Weigh each mouse to determine the appropriate gavage volume. Administer FITC-dextran working solution (50 mg/mL) to each mouse via oral gavage at a dose of 60 mg/100g body weight. Use a sterile, disposable gavage needle appropriate for mouse size (e.g., 20-gauge, 38 mm length, ball-tipped). Ensure proper technique to avoid injury to the esophagus or lungs.
steps.notes: Typical gavage volume for an 25g mouse would be 0.3 mL.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice successfully gavaged with FITC-dextran.
troubleshooting: If mouse chokes or solution enters lungs: Immediately euthanize the mouse and exclude from study.
If gavage needle causes injury: Euthanize the mouse and exclude from study. Review gavage technique.

steps.title: Return Mice to Cages and Monitor
steps.body_md: Immediately after gavage, return mice to their individual cages. Continue to withhold food, but provide free access to water. Monitor mice for any signs of distress or adverse reactions during the post-gavage period.
steps.notes: The 4-hour period allows for absorption of FITC-dextran into the bloodstream.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice remain calm and active after gavage.

steps.title: Terminal Blood Collection
steps.body_md: Four hours after FITC-dextran gavage, perform terminal blood collection. Euthanize mice using a humane method (e.g., CO2 asphyxiation followed by cervical dislocation). Immediately after euthanasia, collect blood via cardiac puncture into a microcentrifuge tube. Collect approximately 0.5-1 mL of blood per mouse.
steps.notes: Prompt blood collection post-euthanasia is crucial to prevent coagulation and degradation of samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Sufficient volume of whole blood (0.5-1 mL) collected from each mouse.
troubleshooting: If blood clots before collection: Ensure rapid collection and consider pre-coating tubes with anticoagulant if needed (though not typically required for rapid terminal collection).
If insufficient blood volume: Review cardiac puncture technique.

steps.title: Process Blood Samples for Plasma
steps.body_md: Immediately after collection, centrifuge the whole blood samples at 2000 x g for 15 minutes at 4°C to separate plasma. Carefully aspirate the supernatant (plasma) using a pipette, avoiding the cellular pellet. Transfer the plasma to a new, labeled microcentrifuge tube.
steps.notes: Maintain cold temperatures throughout to minimize degradation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, yellowish plasma separated from red blood cells. Plasma volume typically ~50% of whole blood volume.
troubleshooting: If plasma is hemolyzed (reddish): This indicates improper blood collection technique or rough handling. Flag sample for potential exclusion or note for analysis.

steps.title: Store Plasma Samples
steps.body_md: Aliquot plasma samples into smaller volumes (e.g., 50-100 µL) in cryovials or microcentrifuge tubes to avoid repeated freeze-thaw cycles. Store aliquoted plasma samples at -80°C until further analysis.
steps.notes: Proper storage ensures the integrity of FITC-dextran for accurate fluorescence measurement.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Plasma samples are properly aliquoted and stored at -80°C.

steps.title: Measure FITC-Dextran Fluorescence
steps.body_md: When ready for analysis, thaw plasma samples on ice. Pipette 50 µL of each plasma sample into a black, clear-bottom 96-well plate. Measure fluorescence intensity using a microplate reader with excitation at 485 nm and emission at 535 nm.
controls: Standard curve of FITC-dextran in untreated mouse plasma
steps.notes: Create a standard curve using known concentrations of FITC-dextran diluted in untreated mouse plasma to accurately quantify FITC-dextran levels in experimental samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Raw fluorescence units obtained for all plasma samples and standards.
troubleshooting: If fluorescence readings are low/high or inconsistent: Verify plate reader settings, check for bubbles in wells, and ensure proper pipetting technique.

steps.title: Calculate Plasma FITC-Dextran Concentration
steps.body_md: Generate a standard curve by plotting the fluorescence intensity of known FITC-dextran concentrations against their respective concentrations. Use this standard curve to calculate the concentration of FITC-dextran (in µg/mL) in each experimental plasma sample.
equipment_needed: Computer with graphing software (e.g., GraphPad Prism, Excel)
todo_for_researcher: Perform linear regression or appropriate curve fitting for standard curve.
steps.notes: The resulting FITC-dextran plasma concentration is a direct measure of intestinal permeability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Quantified FITC-dextran concentration (µg/mL) for each mouse plasma sample.

equipment: Computer with graphing software (e.g., GraphPad Prism, Excel)

success_criteria.how_measured: Fluorescence intensity at 485/535 nm, converted to concentration using a standard curve.
success_criteria.threshold: Measurable FITC-dextran concentration above background in all experimental samples; standard curve R² > 0.98.

success_criteria.what: Absence of hemolysis in plasma samples
success_criteria.how_measured: Visual inspection of plasma samples after centrifugation.
success_criteria.threshold: Plasma should be clear and yellowish, not reddish.

success_criteria.what: Successful oral gavage for all animals
success_criteria.how_measured: Absence of respiratory distress or signs of esophageal injury immediately after gavage.
success_criteria.threshold: All mice successfully gavaged without adverse events.

procedures.name: Tissue Collection and Processing
procedures.intent: To collect intestinal tissue samples for subsequent protein expression analysis.

steps.title: Euthanize mice
steps.body_md: Euthanize mice according to institutional animal care and use committee (IACUC) approved protocols. This typically involves CO2 asphyxiation followed by cervical dislocation or thoracotomy to ensure death.
todo_for_researcher: Refer to institution's specific IACUC protocol for euthanasia.
steps.notes: Ensure humane and rapid euthanasia to minimize stress and degradation of tissue samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Animals are humanely euthanized.

steps.title: Perform laparotomy and expose intestines
steps.body_md: Place the euthanized mouse in a supine position on a dissection tray. Secure the limbs with pins. Using fine dissecting scissors and forceps, make a midline incision through the skin from the sternum to the pelvis. Carefully open the abdominal cavity by making a small incision through the peritoneal wall without damaging the underlying intestines. Extend this incision laterally to expose the entire intestinal tract.
steps.notes: Work quickly but carefully to prevent desiccation of tissues.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Intestinal tract is fully exposed.

steps.title: Isolate the target intestinal segment
steps.body_md: Carefully identify the ileum and colon regions. For the ileum, identify its transition from the jejunum (distal to the duodenum) and its connection to the cecum. For the colon, identify the cecum and rectum. Measure and resect a 2 cm segment of the distal ileum and a 2 cm segment of the mid-colon. Use surgical thread to tie off each end of the selected segment to prevent luminal content spillage.
steps.notes: Maintain consistent length and location for all collected segments across all animals.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Two ~2 cm segments of distal ileum and mid-colon, ligated, are isolated.
troubleshooting: If luminal contents spill: Quickly rinse the segment exterior with ice-cold PBS, but try to avoid this by effective ligation.
If unable to consistently identify regions: Consult an anatomical atlas or experienced researcher.

steps.title: Rinse intestinal segments
steps.body_md: Transfer the resected intestinal segments into a beaker containing 10 mL of ice-cold 1x PBS. Gently wash the segments by swirling for 10-15 seconds to remove any loose luminal contents and blood. Avoid excessive manipulation or squeezing which could damage the tissue.
steps.notes: Use a fresh PBS aliquot for each sample to prevent cross-contamination.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Intestinal segments are clean of external debris and luminal contents.

steps.title: Open and prepare segments for snap freezing
steps.body_md: Transfer each intestinal segment to a clean, paraffin-covered dissection plate on ice. Using fine scissors, carefully cut longitudinally along the mesenteric border of each segment to open it flat. Gently scrape the luminal surface (mucosa) with the blunt side of a scalpel blade to remove any remaining luminal content without damaging the tissue.
steps.notes: Keep all materials on ice to minimize tissue degradation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Intestinal segments are opened flat with clean luminal surfaces.
troubleshooting: If tissue tears during opening: Use gentler pressure and sharper scissors.
If tissue looks 'scraped' or damaged by scalpel: Use the blunt side of the scalpel more gently, or a cotton swab.

steps.title: Snap freeze tissue samples
steps.body_md: Cut each opened intestinal segment into 2-3 small pieces (approx. 5 mm x 5 mm each) suitable for protein extraction. Immediately transfer each piece into a cryo-vial (e.g., 2 mL screw-cap tube) that has been pre-labeled with sample ID. Submerge the cryo-vials directly into liquid nitrogen for snap freezing. Ensure rapid freezing to prevent ice crystal formation.
todo_for_researcher: Determine optimal tissue piece size for downstream protein extraction homogenizer.
steps.notes: Work quickly to minimize thaw-freeze cycles. Each segment will yield multiple samples (e.g., 2-3 ileum pieces, 2-3 colon pieces per animal).
steps.anticipated_outcome: Tissue pieces are rapidly frozen and stored in liquid nitrogen.
troubleshooting: If tissue freezes slowly: Ensure sufficient liquid nitrogen in the dewar and minimize air exposure of tubes.
If tissue pieces are messy or too large: Refine cutting technique on practice tissue.

steps.title: Store samples
steps.body_md: Once snap-frozen, transfer the cryo-vials from liquid nitrogen to a -80°C freezer for long-term storage until protein extraction and Western Blot analysis. Record the storage location for each sample.
steps.notes: Avoid repeated thawing and freezing of samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Frozen tissue samples are securely stored for future analysis.

success_criteria.what: Intestinal tissue samples (distal ileum and mid-colon)
success_criteria.how_measured: Visual inspection of collected segments after processing
success_criteria.threshold: At least 2-3 snap-frozen pieces of approximately 5x5mm each for both ileum and colon, free of visible contamination or damage, for each mouse.

success_criteria.how_measured: Absence of gross signs of degradation (e.g., discoloration, significant ice crystallization indicative of slow freezing)
Proper labeling of cryo-vials and corresponding storage records
success_criteria.threshold: All samples are uniquely identifiable and traceable.

procedures.name: Protein Extraction and Quantification
procedures.intent: To extract total protein from intestinal tissue samples and quantify protein concentration for Western blot analysis.

steps.title: Prepare Lysis Buffer
steps.body_md: Prepare a suitable lysis buffer. For tight junction protein analysis, a RIPA buffer (Radioimmunoprecipitation Assay buffer) containing protease and phosphatase inhibitors is commonly used. Ensure all components are thoroughly mixed.
steps.notes: RIPA buffer formulation can vary slightly; a common formulation includes 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), 150 mM NaCl, 1% NP-40, 0.5% sodium deoxycholate, 0.1% SDS. Add protease and phosphatase inhibitors just before use.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, homogenous lysis buffer solution.

reagent_recipes.name: RIPA Lysis Buffer (with inhibitors)
reagent_recipes.notes: Prepare base RIPA buffer and store at 4°C. Add protease and phosphatase inhibitors fresh before each use according to manufacturer's instructions. Adjust pH to 7.4 with HCl if needed.

steps.title: Homogenize Intestinal Tissue
steps.body_md: Place approximately 50-100 mg of snap-frozen intestinal tissue (e.g., from jejunum or ileum) into a pre-chilled microcentrifuge tube. Add 500 µL of ice-cold lysis buffer (prepared in Step 1). Homogenize the tissue using a mechanical homogenizer (e.g., bead mill or rotor-stator homogenizer) until no visible tissue clumps remain.
equipment_needed: Mechanical homogenizer (e.g., bead mill or rotor-stator)
todo_for_researcher: Determine optimal homogenization time and intensity based on specific homogenizer used to ensure complete lysis without excessive frothing.
steps.notes: Work quickly on ice to minimize protein degradation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Homogenous turbid lysate with no visible tissue pieces.
troubleshooting: If tissue is not fully homogenized: Increase homogenization time or intensity.
If excessive frothing occurs: Reduce homogenization intensity or consider a different homogenizer type (e.g., sonication).

steps.title: Incubate for Lysis
steps.body_md: After homogenization, incubate the samples on ice for 30 minutes, with intermittent vortexing every 5 minutes, to allow for complete lysis of cells and protein extraction.
steps.notes: Keeping samples on ice is crucial to prevent protein degradation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Protein extraction facilitated by lysis buffer action.

steps.title: Centrifuge Cell Lysates
steps.body_md: Centrifuge the lysates at 12,000 x g for 15 minutes at 4°C to pellet cellular debris and insoluble material.
steps.notes: Ensure the centrifuge is pre-cooled to 4°C.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Formation of a pellet at the bottom of the tube; clear supernatant containing soluble protein.
troubleshooting: If supernatant is cloudy: Re-centrifuge for a longer duration or at higher speed.

steps.title: Collect Protein Supernatant
steps.body_md: Carefully transfer the supernatant (containing the total protein extract) to a new, pre-chilled microcentrifuge tube. Avoid disturbing the pellet.
steps.notes: This supernatant is your total protein sample. Label tubes clearly.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear protein solution collected in a new tube.
troubleshooting: If pellet is accidentally aspirated: Re-centrifuge the aspirated material to re-pellet debris.

steps.title: Prepare Protein Standards
steps.body_md: Prepare a serial dilution of Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) standard in lysis buffer, ranging from 0 to 2000 µg/mL. Prepare at least three replicates for each standard concentration.
reagents_referenced: Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) standard (2 mg/mL stock)
steps.notes: Using lysis buffer as the diluent for the standard curve helps account for interference from buffer components.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A series of BSA solutions with known concentrations.

steps.title: Prepare Samples for Quantification
steps.body_md: Dilute a small aliquot (e.g., 10-20 µL) of each protein sample collected in Step 5 in lysis buffer, if necessary, to ensure the concentration falls within the linear range of the standard curve (e.g., 25-1500 µg/mL). Prepare at least three replicates for each sample.
steps.notes: Perform an initial pilot dilution if unsure of sample concentration to avoid out-of-range readings.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Diluted protein samples ready for assay.

steps.title: Perform Protein Quantification (BCA Assay)
steps.body_md: Perform a Bicinchoninic Acid (BCA) protein assay according to the manufacturer's instructions. Mix Reagent A and Reagent B in a 50:1 ratio to create the working reagent. Add a consistent volume (e.g., 25 µL) of each standard and sample (from Steps 6 and 7) into individual wells of a 96-well plate. Add 200 µL of the BCA working reagent to each well. Mix thoroughly by gentle tapping.
reagents_referenced: BCA Protein Assay Kit (Reagent A, Reagent B)
steps.notes: Ensure proper mixing for consistent color development. This step assumes a BCA assay; if using Bradford, follow that kit's specific instructions.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Standards and samples mixed with colorimetric reagent.
troubleshooting: If color development is uneven: Ensure thorough mixing of working reagent and sample/standard in each well.
If results are outside linear range: Adjust sample dilution and re-run the assay.

steps.title: Incubate BCA Assay Plate
steps.body_md: Incubate the 96-well plate at 37°C for 30 minutes. This incubation allows color development proportional to the protein concentration.
equipment_needed: Plate incubator (e.g., microplate reader with heating function)
steps.notes: Some BCA protocols recommend room temperature incubation, but 37°C often accelerates color development for denser plates.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Solutions in wells will change color (purple) according to protein concentration.
troubleshooting: If color does not develop: Check shelf life of BCA reagents, ensure correct mixing ratio of Reagent A and B.

steps.title: Read Absorbance
steps.body_md: Cool the plate to room temperature and then immediately measure the absorbance at 562 nm using a plate reader.
steps.notes: Reading promptly after incubation prevents over-development or fading of color.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Absorbance readings for all standards and samples.
troubleshooting: If readings are not consistent across replicates: Re-evaluate pipetting technique and mixing steps.

steps.title: Calculate Protein Concentrations
steps.body_md: Generate a standard curve by plotting the average absorbance values of the BSA standards against their known concentrations. Use this standard curve to determine the protein concentration of each experimental sample.
equipment_needed: Computer with graphing software (e.g., Excel, GraphPad Prism)
steps.notes: A linear regression fit is typically used for the standard curve. Ensure the R-squared value is acceptable (>0.98).
steps.anticipated_outcome: Quantified protein concentrations (µg/mL) for all intestinal tissue samples.
troubleshooting: If R-squared is low for standard curve: Review standard preparation and pipetting accuracy; consider re-running the assay for standards.

steps.title: Store Protein Samples
steps.body_md: Aliquot the remaining protein extracts into single-use aliquots. Store at -80°C until further use for Western blot analysis.
steps.notes: Avoid multiple freeze-thaw cycles as this can degrade proteins.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Frozen, aliquoted protein samples ready for long-term storage.

equipment: Mechanical homogenizer (e.g., bead mill or rotor-stator)
Computer with graphing software (e.g., Excel, GraphPad Prism)
reagents: Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) standard (2 mg/mL stock)
BCA Protein Assay Kit (Reagent A, Reagent B)

success_criteria.how_measured: R-squared value of the BSA standard curve
Coefficient of variation (CV) for replicate samples

success_criteria.what: Protein concentration range for experimental samples
success_criteria.threshold: Appropriate for downstream Western blot (e.g., 0.5-5 µg/µL)

procedures.name: Western Blot for Tight Junction Proteins
procedures.intent: To quantify the relative expression levels of claudin-1 and occludin in intestinal tissue samples.

steps.title: Prepare Intestinal Tissue Lysates
steps.body_md: Homogenize frozen intestinal tissue samples (approximately 50-100 mg per sample) in 500 µL of RIPA lysis buffer supplemented with protease and phosphatase inhibitor cocktails. Use a bead mill homogenizer for 2 cycles of 30 seconds at 4.0 m/s, with a 30-second pause on ice between cycles. Keep samples on ice throughout the process.
steps.notes: Ensure all reagents are cold prior to use to minimize protein degradation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Homogenized tissue suspension, ready for centrifugation.
troubleshooting: If tissue is not fully homogenized: Increase homogenization cycles or duration, ensuring samples remain cold.
If protein degradation is suspected: Add cOmplete protease inhibitor cocktail freshly before use.

reagent_recipes.name: RIPA Lysis Buffer
reagent_recipes.notes: Store at 4°C. Add protease and phosphatase inhibitors immediately before use.

steps.title: Clarify Lysates by Centrifugation
steps.body_md: Centrifuge the homogenized samples at 12,000 x g for 15 minutes at 4°C to pellet cellular debris. Carefully transfer the supernatant (containing the protein lysate) to a new, pre-chilled microcentrifuge tube.
steps.notes: Avoid disturbing the pellet when collecting the supernatant.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear supernatant, free of particulate matter.
troubleshooting: If supernatant is cloudy: Re-centrifuge for a longer duration or at a higher speed.
If pellet is accidentally disturbed: Re-centrifuge the sample.

steps.title: Determine Protein Concentration
steps.body_md: Determine the protein concentration of each lysate using a BCA protein assay kit according to the manufacturer's instructions. Prepare a standard curve using bovine serum albumin (BSA).
steps.notes: Ensure assay is set up and read within the recommended time frame for accurate results.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Protein concentrations for all samples, typically ranging from 1-10 mg/mL.
troubleshooting: If readings are outside standard curve: Dilute or concentrate samples and repeat the assay.

steps.title: Prepare Samples for SDS-PAGE
steps.body_md: Dilute each protein lysate to a uniform concentration (e.g., 20 µg/lane) in 1x Laemmli sample buffer containing 2-mercaptoethanol. Heat samples at 95°C for 5 minutes to denature proteins.
steps.notes: The 2-mercaptoethanol is crucial for reducing disulfide bonds.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Denatured protein samples, ready for loading.

steps.title: Perform SDS-PAGE
steps.body_md: Load 20 µg of denatured protein from each sample onto a 10% SDS-polyacrylamide gel (Mini-PROTEAN TGX Stain-Free Gels, Bio-Rad). Include a pre-stained protein ladder in one lane. Run the gel at 120 V for approximately 60-90 minutes, or until the dye front reaches the bottom of the gel.
steps.notes: Ensure proper seating of the gel cassette to prevent leaks.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Separated proteins within the gel, visible by the protein ladder.
troubleshooting: If bands are smeared: Reduce voltage or ensure buffer is fresh.
If no separation: Check power supply and buffer levels.

steps.title: Transfer Proteins to PVDF Membrane
steps.body_md: Transfer the separated proteins from the SDS-PAGE gel to a PVDF membrane using a wet transfer system (e.g., Bio-Rad Mini Trans-Blot® Cell). Equilibrate the membrane in methanol for 30 seconds, then assemble the transfer sandwich. Transfer at 100 V for 60 minutes at 4°C with constant stirring.
steps.notes: Ensure no air bubbles are trapped between the gel and the membrane during sandwich assembly. PVDF membrane must be pre-wetted in methanol.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Proteins successfully transferred to the PVDF membrane.
troubleshooting: If transfer is incomplete: Increase transfer time or voltage, ensure buffer is fresh and cold.
If membrane shows white spots: Re-wet membrane thoroughly in methanol before assembly.

reagent_recipes.name: Transfer Buffer (Wester Blot)
reagent_recipes.notes: Do not adjust pH. Store at 4°C.

steps.title: Block Membrane
steps.body_md: Block the PVDF membrane in 5% non-fat dry milk (NFDM) dissolved in Tris-Buffered Saline with Tween® 20 (TBST) for 1 hour at room temperature with gentle shaking.
steps.notes: Blocking prevents non-specific antibody binding.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Membrane uniformly coated with blocking solution.
troubleshooting: If high background is observed: Increase blocking time or concentration of NFDM.

reagent_recipes.name: TBST (Tris-Buffered Saline with Tween® 20)
reagent_recipes.notes: Store at room temperature. Adjust pH to 7.5.

steps.title: Incubate with Primary Antibodies
steps.body_md: Incubate the membrane overnight at 4°C with the primary antibodies diluted in 5% BSA in TBST. Use the following dilutions: anti-claudin-1 (1:1000), anti-occludin (1:1000), and anti-GAPDH or anti-beta-actin (1:5000) as a loading control.
steps.notes: Use separate containers or strip and re-probe membrane if using multiple primary antibodies with similar molecular weights.
troubleshooting: If no signal: Check antibody dilutions, ensure primary antibody was added.
If high background: Increase blocking time or consider higher purity BSA.

steps.title: Wash Membrane
steps.body_md: Wash the membrane three times for 10 minutes each with TBST at room temperature with gentle shaking.
steps.notes: Thorough washing crucial for reducing background noise.
troubleshooting: If high background persists: Increase wash duration or number of washes.

steps.title: Incubate with Secondary Antibodies
steps.body_md: Incubate the membrane with horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-conjugated secondary antibodies diluted 1:5000 in 5% NFDM in TBST for 1 hour at room temperature with gentle shaking.
reagents_referenced: HRP-conjugated secondary antibody (anti-rabbit or anti-mouse, depending on primary host)
steps.notes: Protect secondary antibody solution from strong light.
troubleshooting: If high background on control lanes: Consider reducing secondary antibody concentration.

steps.title: Final Washes
steps.body_md: Wash the membrane four times for 10 minutes each with TBST at room temperature with gentle shaking.
steps.notes: These washes are critical for removing unbound secondary antibody and reducing background noise.
troubleshooting: If background remains high: Increase wash duration or number of washes.

steps.title: Detect Chemiluminescence
steps.body_md: Incubate the membrane with enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) substrate for 1-5 minutes, following the manufacturer's recommendations. Immediately after incubation, capture images using a chemiluminescence imaging system (e.g., Bio-Rad ChemiDoc MP Imaging System). Adjust exposure time for optimal signal without saturation.
steps.notes: Work quickly to prevent substrate exhaustion and ensure consistent signal across samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Visible protein bands corresponding to claudin-1, occludin, and loading control.
troubleshooting: If signal is too weak: Increase exposure time or ensure the ECL reagents are fresh.
If signal is saturated: Reduce exposure time or dilute samples more for future experiments.

steps.title: Quantify Band Intensity
steps.body_md: Quantify the band intensity using image analysis software (e.g., Image Lab software, Bio-Rad). Normalize the intensity of claudin-1 and occludin bands to the intensity of the GAPDH or beta-actin loading control band for each sample.
steps.notes: Ensure consistent region of interest (ROI) selection for all bands during quantification.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Normalized relative protein expression values for claudin-1 and occludin for all samples.

success_criteria.what: Successful protein transfer and antibody binding
success_criteria.how_measured: Visible bands for all target proteins (claudin-1, occludin, and loading control) on the developed membrane without excessive background.
success_criteria.threshold: Clear, distinct bands for at least 80% of samples and controls.

success_criteria.how_measured: Detection and quantification of band intensities using image analysis software, normalized to loading control.
success_criteria.threshold: All samples yield quantifiable relative expression values.

steps.title: Receive and Unpack Mice
steps.body_md: Upon arrival, carefully unpack the C57BL/6 mice from their shipping containers. Visually inspect all mice for signs of stress, injury, or disease (e.g., lethargy, ruffled fur, wounds). Record any observations. Transfer mice to clean, standard caging.
steps.notes: Ensure vivarium staff are aware of the arrival time and have prepared the necessary cages and supplies.
steps.anticipated_outcome: All mice are safely transferred to initial housing, appearing healthy.
troubleshooting: If signs of illness or injury are observed: Segregate affected mice immediately and consult a veterinarian. Document all incidents.
If fewer mice than expected are received: Contact the supplier and vivarium management immediately.

steps.title: Initial Housing Setup
steps.body_md: House the C57BL/6 mice (age 8-10 weeks) under standard vivarium conditions. Ensure a 12-hour light/12-hour dark cycle (e.g., lights on 07:00, lights off 19:00), ambient temperature of 20-24°C, and humidity of 40-60%. Provide ad libitum access to standard chow diet and filtered water.
steps.notes: Record cage IDs, number of mice per cage (initially), and date of housing.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice are housed in appropriate, controlled conditions.

steps.title: Acclimation Period
steps.body_md: Allow mice to acclimate to the vivarium environment for a minimum of 7 days following arrival. During this period, observe mice daily for health, activity, and feeding/drinking behavior. Minimize disturbances to reduce stress.
todo_for_researcher: Specify exact start and end dates for acclimation.
steps.notes: Acclimation helps reduce stress variability between animals prior to experimental interventions.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice appear healthy, active, and have adapted to the new environment, showing no signs of stress or illness.
troubleshooting: If mice show prolonged signs of stress or illness: Extend the acclimation period, re-evaluate environmental conditions, or consult with vivarium veterinarian. Do not proceed to experimental procedures until all mice are healthy and acclimated.

steps.title: Random Group Assignment
steps.body_md: After the acclimation period, randomly assign the C57BL/6 mice to experimental groups: 'L. rhamnosus GG Supplementation' and 'Vehicle Control'. Ensure a minimum of 8 mice per group (n>=8). Use a computer-generated randomization method (e.g., random number generator) to assign individual mice to groups, ensuring an even distribution of initial body weights if possible, but strict randomization is prioritized.
todo_for_researcher: Specify the exact randomization method to be used (e.g., Python script, online calculator).
steps.notes: Record the individual mouse ID and its assigned group. Consider cage effects; ideally, distribute mice from original cages across various experimental groups.
steps.anticipated_outcome: All mice are assigned to either the experimental or control group, with an equal number of mice per group (or as close as possible for odd numbers).
troubleshooting: If randomization results in significant weight disparities between groups: Re-run randomization or stratify by weight if an equal number of cages for each condition is not feasible, though strict randomization is preferred. Document the decision.

steps.title: Individual Housing and Identification
steps.body_md: Individually house mice within their assigned experimental groups to prevent cross-contamination of oral supplements. Assign unique identifiers (e.g., ear tags, microchips, tail marks) to each mouse. Update records to include group assignment and individual identifier for each mouse.
equipment_needed: Ear tag applicator and tags OR Microchip reader and transponders OR Animal markers
todo_for_researcher: Confirm preferred method of individual mouse identification (e.g., ear tag, microchip, temporary tail marking).
steps.notes: Individual housing ensures accurate dosing and avoids potential social interactions influencing permeability or tight junction protein expression.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Each mouse is individually housed and clearly identifiable with its assigned group.
troubleshooting: If a mouse loses its identification: Immediately re-identify the mouse, or if identification is impossible, remove the mouse from the study and document. Ensure backup identification methods are in place for safety.

steps.title: Prepare L. rhamnosus GG (LrGG) Oral Suspension
steps.body_md: Prepare a suspension of Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LrGG) at the specified concentration in filter-sterilized normal saline. The concentration should be sufficient to deliver the target CFU/dose in a small, administrable volume (e.g., 200 µL). Invert gently to mix, do not vortex aggressively.
todo_for_researcher: Determine the exact CFU/mL required based on desired dose and administration volume (e.g., if target is 1x10^9 CFU/mouse/day in 200 uL, then prepare 5x10^9 CFU/mL stock).
steps.notes: LrGG should be obtained as a lyophilized powder or frozen stock. A fresh suspension should be prepared daily to ensure viability and consistent dosing. Ensure aseptic technique during preparation to prevent contamination.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A homogeneous, milky-white suspension with no visible clumps.
troubleshooting: If suspension is clumpy: Ensure proper resuspension technique; allow lyophilized powder to rehydrate for a few minutes before gentle mixing.
If concentration is inconsistent: Verify counting method (e.g., plate counts) and preparation accuracy.

steps.title: Prepare Vehicle Control Oral Solution
steps.body_md: Prepare filter-sterilized normal saline (0.9% NaCl) to be used as the vehicle control. This solution will be orally administered to the control group in the same volume as the LrGG suspension.
steps.notes: Use the same lot of saline for both experimental and control preparations if possible to minimize variability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile saline solution.

steps.title: Restrain Mice for Oral Gavage
steps.body_md: Gently habituate mice to handling for at least 3-5 days before the start of supplementation. For daily gavage, carefully remove one mouse from its cage. Hold the mouse firmly by the scruff of its neck, ensuring its head is slightly elevated and its body is straight. This extends the neck and aligns the esophagus for easier gavage. Support the body with the other hand.
steps.notes: Proper restraint is crucial for safe and effective gavage. Practice on non-experimental mice or with a non-gavage procedure first if inexperienced. Minimize stress to the animals.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mouse is securely and comfortably restrained, ready for gavage.
troubleshooting: If mouse struggles excessively: Release, allow the mouse to calm, and reattempt. Ensure proper scruffing technique to avoid airway compression.
If mouse bites: Use bite guards or wear thicker gloves, ensure adequate scruffing to prevent head movement.

steps.title: Administer Oral Gavage (LrGG or Vehicle)
steps.body_md: Use a sterile, blunt-ended gavage needle or flexible gavage catheter of appropriate size (e.g., 20-gauge or 22-gauge for adult mice). Slowly and steadily insert the gavage needle into the mouse's mouth, guiding it along the palate and down the esophagus. It is crucial to ensure the needle enters the esophagus and not the trachea. A common sign of correct placement is the mouse's ability to swallow. Once the needle is properly positioned, slowly dispense 200 µL of the assigned solution (LrGG suspension for the study group, normal saline for the control group) over 5-10 seconds. Remove the needle gently.
todo_for_researcher: Ensure gavage needle size is appropriate for C57BL/6 mice (e.g., 20GA or 22GA, 38mm length, ball-tipped).
steps.notes: The source protocol administered 5 times/week. However, the hypothesis states 'daily oral supplementation'. This procedure adapts to daily administration. Listen for breathing sounds; if gurgling or wheezing occurs, the needle may be in the trachea. Do not proceed with injection if tracheal placement is suspected.
steps.anticipated_outcome: All mice in their respective groups receive their assigned daily oral dose.
troubleshooting: If fluid enters the trachea (mouse chokes, turns blue): Immediately stop procedure, humanely euthanize the animal, and note incidence. Review gavage technique.
If mouse expels fluid: Confirm proper gavage technique, ensure slow administration. Re-gavage if a significant amount is expelled and animal's condition allows.

steps.title: Return Mouse to Cage and Monitor
steps.body_md: After successful oral gavage, gently return the mouse to its home cage. Briefly observe the mouse for any signs of immediate distress (e.g., labored breathing, lethargy, ruffling of fur). Ensure it returns to normal activity within a few minutes.
steps.notes: Careful monitoring helps ensure animal welfare and identify any issues arising from the gavage procedure.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mouse appears healthy and returns to normal activity within minutes of gavage.
troubleshooting: If mouse exhibits prolonged distress: Humanely euthanize if severe; if mild, monitor closely for the next few hours and consult with veterinary staff.

steps.title: Repeat Daily for 4 Weeks
steps.body_md: Repeat steps 3-5 daily for 4 consecutive weeks for both the LrGG supplementation group and the vehicle control group. Ensure each mouse receives its assigned treatment consistently at approximately the same time each day.
steps.notes: Maintain a detailed record of daily gavages for each mouse, including any missed doses or adverse reactions.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice receive consistent daily dosing over the 4-week study period.
troubleshooting: If a dose is missed: Record the missed dose. Do not double-dose the next day. Continue with the regular schedule.
If daily schedule is difficult to maintain: Administer within a consistent window (e.g., +/- 1 hour from target time).

steps.title: Prepare FITC-Dextran Stock Solution
steps.body_md: Prepare a 100 mg/mL stock solution of FITC-dextran (4 kDa) by dissolving 1 gram of FITC-dextran in 10 mL of sterile PBS. Mix thoroughly by vortexing until completely dissolved. Filter sterilize the solution using a 0.22 µm syringe filter to remove any particulate matter and ensure sterility.
steps.notes: Prepare this stock solution fresh on the day of experiments, or aliquot and store at -20°C for up to one month. Thaw on ice if using frozen stock.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, yellowish-green solution free of particulates.

steps.title: Prepare Working Solution of FITC-Dextran
steps.body_md: Dilute the 100 mg/mL FITC-dextran stock solution with sterile PBS to achieve a working concentration of 50 mg/mL. For example, if preparing 5 mL of working solution, combine 2.5 mL of stock solution with 2.5 mL of sterile PBS. Mix gently.
steps.notes: The final gavaged dose will be 60 mg/100g body weight, assuming a gavage volume of 0.12 mL/10g body weight.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, yellowish-green solution at the desired working concentration.

steps.title: Fast Mice Prior to Gavage
steps.body_md: Remove food from animal cages 4 hours prior to the scheduled gavage time. Provide free access to water during the fasting period.
steps.notes: Fasting ensures consistent gastric emptying and absorption of FITC-dextran.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice appear normal but unfed.

steps.title: Administer FITC-Dextran by Oral Gavage
steps.body_md: Weigh each mouse to determine the appropriate gavage volume. Administer FITC-dextran working solution (50 mg/mL) to each mouse via oral gavage at a dose of 60 mg/100g body weight. Use a sterile, disposable gavage needle appropriate for mouse size (e.g., 20-gauge, 38 mm length, ball-tipped). Ensure proper technique to avoid injury to the esophagus or lungs.
steps.notes: Typical gavage volume for an 25g mouse would be 0.3 mL.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice successfully gavaged with FITC-dextran.
troubleshooting: If mouse chokes or solution enters lungs: Immediately euthanize the mouse and exclude from study.
If gavage needle causes injury: Euthanize the mouse and exclude from study. Review gavage technique.

steps.title: Return Mice to Cages and Monitor
steps.body_md: Immediately after gavage, return mice to their individual cages. Continue to withhold food, but provide free access to water. Monitor mice for any signs of distress or adverse reactions during the post-gavage period.
steps.notes: The 4-hour period allows for absorption of FITC-dextran into the bloodstream.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Mice remain calm and active after gavage.

steps.title: Terminal Blood Collection
steps.body_md: Four hours after FITC-dextran gavage, perform terminal blood collection. Euthanize mice using a humane method (e.g., CO2 asphyxiation followed by cervical dislocation). Immediately after euthanasia, collect blood via cardiac puncture into a microcentrifuge tube. Collect approximately 0.5-1 mL of blood per mouse.
steps.notes: Prompt blood collection post-euthanasia is crucial to prevent coagulation and degradation of samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Sufficient volume of whole blood (0.5-1 mL) collected from each mouse.
troubleshooting: If blood clots before collection: Ensure rapid collection and consider pre-coating tubes with anticoagulant if needed (though not typically required for rapid terminal collection).
If insufficient blood volume: Review cardiac puncture technique.

steps.title: Process Blood Samples for Plasma
steps.body_md: Immediately after collection, centrifuge the whole blood samples at 2000 x g for 15 minutes at 4°C to separate plasma. Carefully aspirate the supernatant (plasma) using a pipette, avoiding the cellular pellet. Transfer the plasma to a new, labeled microcentrifuge tube.
steps.notes: Maintain cold temperatures throughout to minimize degradation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, yellowish plasma separated from red blood cells. Plasma volume typically ~50% of whole blood volume.
troubleshooting: If plasma is hemolyzed (reddish): This indicates improper blood collection technique or rough handling. Flag sample for potential exclusion or note for analysis.

steps.title: Store Plasma Samples
steps.body_md: Aliquot plasma samples into smaller volumes (e.g., 50-100 µL) in cryovials or microcentrifuge tubes to avoid repeated freeze-thaw cycles. Store aliquoted plasma samples at -80°C until further analysis.
steps.notes: Proper storage ensures the integrity of FITC-dextran for accurate fluorescence measurement.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Plasma samples are properly aliquoted and stored at -80°C.

steps.title: Measure FITC-Dextran Fluorescence
steps.body_md: When ready for analysis, thaw plasma samples on ice. Pipette 50 µL of each plasma sample into a black, clear-bottom 96-well plate. Measure fluorescence intensity using a microplate reader with excitation at 485 nm and emission at 535 nm.
controls: Standard curve of FITC-dextran in untreated mouse plasma
steps.notes: Create a standard curve using known concentrations of FITC-dextran diluted in untreated mouse plasma to accurately quantify FITC-dextran levels in experimental samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Raw fluorescence units obtained for all plasma samples and standards.
troubleshooting: If fluorescence readings are low/high or inconsistent: Verify plate reader settings, check for bubbles in wells, and ensure proper pipetting technique.

steps.title: Calculate Plasma FITC-Dextran Concentration
steps.body_md: Generate a standard curve by plotting the fluorescence intensity of known FITC-dextran concentrations against their respective concentrations. Use this standard curve to calculate the concentration of FITC-dextran (in µg/mL) in each experimental plasma sample.
equipment_needed: Computer with graphing software (e.g., GraphPad Prism, Excel)
todo_for_researcher: Perform linear regression or appropriate curve fitting for standard curve.
steps.notes: The resulting FITC-dextran plasma concentration is a direct measure of intestinal permeability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Quantified FITC-dextran concentration (µg/mL) for each mouse plasma sample.

steps.title: Euthanize mice
steps.body_md: Euthanize mice according to institutional animal care and use committee (IACUC) approved protocols. This typically involves CO2 asphyxiation followed by cervical dislocation or thoracotomy to ensure death.
todo_for_researcher: Refer to institution's specific IACUC protocol for euthanasia.
steps.notes: Ensure humane and rapid euthanasia to minimize stress and degradation of tissue samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Animals are humanely euthanized.

steps.title: Perform laparotomy and expose intestines
steps.body_md: Place the euthanized mouse in a supine position on a dissection tray. Secure the limbs with pins. Using fine dissecting scissors and forceps, make a midline incision through the skin from the sternum to the pelvis. Carefully open the abdominal cavity by making a small incision through the peritoneal wall without damaging the underlying intestines. Extend this incision laterally to expose the entire intestinal tract.
steps.notes: Work quickly but carefully to prevent desiccation of tissues.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Intestinal tract is fully exposed.

steps.title: Isolate the target intestinal segment
steps.body_md: Carefully identify the ileum and colon regions. For the ileum, identify its transition from the jejunum (distal to the duodenum) and its connection to the cecum. For the colon, identify the cecum and rectum. Measure and resect a 2 cm segment of the distal ileum and a 2 cm segment of the mid-colon. Use surgical thread to tie off each end of the selected segment to prevent luminal content spillage.
steps.notes: Maintain consistent length and location for all collected segments across all animals.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Two ~2 cm segments of distal ileum and mid-colon, ligated, are isolated.
troubleshooting: If luminal contents spill: Quickly rinse the segment exterior with ice-cold PBS, but try to avoid this by effective ligation.
If unable to consistently identify regions: Consult an anatomical atlas or experienced researcher.

steps.title: Rinse intestinal segments
steps.body_md: Transfer the resected intestinal segments into a beaker containing 10 mL of ice-cold 1x PBS. Gently wash the segments by swirling for 10-15 seconds to remove any loose luminal contents and blood. Avoid excessive manipulation or squeezing which could damage the tissue.
steps.notes: Use a fresh PBS aliquot for each sample to prevent cross-contamination.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Intestinal segments are clean of external debris and luminal contents.

steps.title: Open and prepare segments for snap freezing
steps.body_md: Transfer each intestinal segment to a clean, paraffin-covered dissection plate on ice. Using fine scissors, carefully cut longitudinally along the mesenteric border of each segment to open it flat. Gently scrape the luminal surface (mucosa) with the blunt side of a scalpel blade to remove any remaining luminal content without damaging the tissue.
steps.notes: Keep all materials on ice to minimize tissue degradation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Intestinal segments are opened flat with clean luminal surfaces.
troubleshooting: If tissue tears during opening: Use gentler pressure and sharper scissors.
If tissue looks 'scraped' or damaged by scalpel: Use the blunt side of the scalpel more gently, or a cotton swab.

steps.title: Snap freeze tissue samples
steps.body_md: Cut each opened intestinal segment into 2-3 small pieces (approx. 5 mm x 5 mm each) suitable for protein extraction. Immediately transfer each piece into a cryo-vial (e.g., 2 mL screw-cap tube) that has been pre-labeled with sample ID. Submerge the cryo-vials directly into liquid nitrogen for snap freezing. Ensure rapid freezing to prevent ice crystal formation.
todo_for_researcher: Determine optimal tissue piece size for downstream protein extraction homogenizer.
steps.notes: Work quickly to minimize thaw-freeze cycles. Each segment will yield multiple samples (e.g., 2-3 ileum pieces, 2-3 colon pieces per animal).
steps.anticipated_outcome: Tissue pieces are rapidly frozen and stored in liquid nitrogen.
troubleshooting: If tissue freezes slowly: Ensure sufficient liquid nitrogen in the dewar and minimize air exposure of tubes.
If tissue pieces are messy or too large: Refine cutting technique on practice tissue.

steps.title: Store samples
steps.body_md: Once snap-frozen, transfer the cryo-vials from liquid nitrogen to a -80°C freezer for long-term storage until protein extraction and Western Blot analysis. Record the storage location for each sample.
steps.notes: Avoid repeated thawing and freezing of samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Frozen tissue samples are securely stored for future analysis.

steps.title: Prepare Lysis Buffer
steps.body_md: Prepare a suitable lysis buffer. For tight junction protein analysis, a RIPA buffer (Radioimmunoprecipitation Assay buffer) containing protease and phosphatase inhibitors is commonly used. Ensure all components are thoroughly mixed.
steps.notes: RIPA buffer formulation can vary slightly; a common formulation includes 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), 150 mM NaCl, 1% NP-40, 0.5% sodium deoxycholate, 0.1% SDS. Add protease and phosphatase inhibitors just before use.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, homogenous lysis buffer solution.

reagent_recipes.name: RIPA Lysis Buffer (with inhibitors)
reagent_recipes.notes: Prepare base RIPA buffer and store at 4°C. Add protease and phosphatase inhibitors fresh before each use according to manufacturer's instructions. Adjust pH to 7.4 with HCl if needed.

steps.title: Homogenize Intestinal Tissue
steps.body_md: Place approximately 50-100 mg of snap-frozen intestinal tissue (e.g., from jejunum or ileum) into a pre-chilled microcentrifuge tube. Add 500 µL of ice-cold lysis buffer (prepared in Step 1). Homogenize the tissue using a mechanical homogenizer (e.g., bead mill or rotor-stator homogenizer) until no visible tissue clumps remain.
equipment_needed: Mechanical homogenizer (e.g., bead mill or rotor-stator)
todo_for_researcher: Determine optimal homogenization time and intensity based on specific homogenizer used to ensure complete lysis without excessive frothing.
steps.notes: Work quickly on ice to minimize protein degradation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Homogenous turbid lysate with no visible tissue pieces.
troubleshooting: If tissue is not fully homogenized: Increase homogenization time or intensity.
If excessive frothing occurs: Reduce homogenization intensity or consider a different homogenizer type (e.g., sonication).

steps.title: Incubate for Lysis
steps.body_md: After homogenization, incubate the samples on ice for 30 minutes, with intermittent vortexing every 5 minutes, to allow for complete lysis of cells and protein extraction.
steps.notes: Keeping samples on ice is crucial to prevent protein degradation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Protein extraction facilitated by lysis buffer action.

steps.title: Centrifuge Cell Lysates
steps.body_md: Centrifuge the lysates at 12,000 x g for 15 minutes at 4°C to pellet cellular debris and insoluble material.
steps.notes: Ensure the centrifuge is pre-cooled to 4°C.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Formation of a pellet at the bottom of the tube; clear supernatant containing soluble protein.
troubleshooting: If supernatant is cloudy: Re-centrifuge for a longer duration or at higher speed.

steps.title: Collect Protein Supernatant
steps.body_md: Carefully transfer the supernatant (containing the total protein extract) to a new, pre-chilled microcentrifuge tube. Avoid disturbing the pellet.
steps.notes: This supernatant is your total protein sample. Label tubes clearly.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear protein solution collected in a new tube.
troubleshooting: If pellet is accidentally aspirated: Re-centrifuge the aspirated material to re-pellet debris.

steps.title: Prepare Protein Standards
steps.body_md: Prepare a serial dilution of Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) standard in lysis buffer, ranging from 0 to 2000 µg/mL. Prepare at least three replicates for each standard concentration.
reagents_referenced: Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) standard (2 mg/mL stock)
steps.notes: Using lysis buffer as the diluent for the standard curve helps account for interference from buffer components.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A series of BSA solutions with known concentrations.

steps.title: Prepare Samples for Quantification
steps.body_md: Dilute a small aliquot (e.g., 10-20 µL) of each protein sample collected in Step 5 in lysis buffer, if necessary, to ensure the concentration falls within the linear range of the standard curve (e.g., 25-1500 µg/mL). Prepare at least three replicates for each sample.
steps.notes: Perform an initial pilot dilution if unsure of sample concentration to avoid out-of-range readings.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Diluted protein samples ready for assay.

steps.title: Perform Protein Quantification (BCA Assay)
steps.body_md: Perform a Bicinchoninic Acid (BCA) protein assay according to the manufacturer's instructions. Mix Reagent A and Reagent B in a 50:1 ratio to create the working reagent. Add a consistent volume (e.g., 25 µL) of each standard and sample (from Steps 6 and 7) into individual wells of a 96-well plate. Add 200 µL of the BCA working reagent to each well. Mix thoroughly by gentle tapping.
reagents_referenced: BCA Protein Assay Kit (Reagent A, Reagent B)
steps.notes: Ensure proper mixing for consistent color development. This step assumes a BCA assay; if using Bradford, follow that kit's specific instructions.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Standards and samples mixed with colorimetric reagent.
troubleshooting: If color development is uneven: Ensure thorough mixing of working reagent and sample/standard in each well.
If results are outside linear range: Adjust sample dilution and re-run the assay.

steps.title: Incubate BCA Assay Plate
steps.body_md: Incubate the 96-well plate at 37°C for 30 minutes. This incubation allows color development proportional to the protein concentration.
equipment_needed: Plate incubator (e.g., microplate reader with heating function)
steps.notes: Some BCA protocols recommend room temperature incubation, but 37°C often accelerates color development for denser plates.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Solutions in wells will change color (purple) according to protein concentration.
troubleshooting: If color does not develop: Check shelf life of BCA reagents, ensure correct mixing ratio of Reagent A and B.

steps.title: Read Absorbance
steps.body_md: Cool the plate to room temperature and then immediately measure the absorbance at 562 nm using a plate reader.
steps.notes: Reading promptly after incubation prevents over-development or fading of color.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Absorbance readings for all standards and samples.
troubleshooting: If readings are not consistent across replicates: Re-evaluate pipetting technique and mixing steps.

steps.title: Calculate Protein Concentrations
steps.body_md: Generate a standard curve by plotting the average absorbance values of the BSA standards against their known concentrations. Use this standard curve to determine the protein concentration of each experimental sample.
equipment_needed: Computer with graphing software (e.g., Excel, GraphPad Prism)
steps.notes: A linear regression fit is typically used for the standard curve. Ensure the R-squared value is acceptable (>0.98).
steps.anticipated_outcome: Quantified protein concentrations (µg/mL) for all intestinal tissue samples.
troubleshooting: If R-squared is low for standard curve: Review standard preparation and pipetting accuracy; consider re-running the assay for standards.

steps.title: Store Protein Samples
steps.body_md: Aliquot the remaining protein extracts into single-use aliquots. Store at -80°C until further use for Western blot analysis.
steps.notes: Avoid multiple freeze-thaw cycles as this can degrade proteins.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Frozen, aliquoted protein samples ready for long-term storage.

steps.title: Prepare Intestinal Tissue Lysates
steps.body_md: Homogenize frozen intestinal tissue samples (approximately 50-100 mg per sample) in 500 µL of RIPA lysis buffer supplemented with protease and phosphatase inhibitor cocktails. Use a bead mill homogenizer for 2 cycles of 30 seconds at 4.0 m/s, with a 30-second pause on ice between cycles. Keep samples on ice throughout the process.
steps.notes: Ensure all reagents are cold prior to use to minimize protein degradation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Homogenized tissue suspension, ready for centrifugation.
troubleshooting: If tissue is not fully homogenized: Increase homogenization cycles or duration, ensuring samples remain cold.
If protein degradation is suspected: Add cOmplete protease inhibitor cocktail freshly before use.

reagent_recipes.name: RIPA Lysis Buffer
reagent_recipes.notes: Store at 4°C. Add protease and phosphatase inhibitors immediately before use.

steps.title: Clarify Lysates by Centrifugation
steps.body_md: Centrifuge the homogenized samples at 12,000 x g for 15 minutes at 4°C to pellet cellular debris. Carefully transfer the supernatant (containing the protein lysate) to a new, pre-chilled microcentrifuge tube.
steps.notes: Avoid disturbing the pellet when collecting the supernatant.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear supernatant, free of particulate matter.
troubleshooting: If supernatant is cloudy: Re-centrifuge for a longer duration or at a higher speed.
If pellet is accidentally disturbed: Re-centrifuge the sample.

steps.title: Determine Protein Concentration
steps.body_md: Determine the protein concentration of each lysate using a BCA protein assay kit according to the manufacturer's instructions. Prepare a standard curve using bovine serum albumin (BSA).
steps.notes: Ensure assay is set up and read within the recommended time frame for accurate results.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Protein concentrations for all samples, typically ranging from 1-10 mg/mL.
troubleshooting: If readings are outside standard curve: Dilute or concentrate samples and repeat the assay.

steps.title: Prepare Samples for SDS-PAGE
steps.body_md: Dilute each protein lysate to a uniform concentration (e.g., 20 µg/lane) in 1x Laemmli sample buffer containing 2-mercaptoethanol. Heat samples at 95°C for 5 minutes to denature proteins.
steps.notes: The 2-mercaptoethanol is crucial for reducing disulfide bonds.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Denatured protein samples, ready for loading.

steps.title: Perform SDS-PAGE
steps.body_md: Load 20 µg of denatured protein from each sample onto a 10% SDS-polyacrylamide gel (Mini-PROTEAN TGX Stain-Free Gels, Bio-Rad). Include a pre-stained protein ladder in one lane. Run the gel at 120 V for approximately 60-90 minutes, or until the dye front reaches the bottom of the gel.
steps.notes: Ensure proper seating of the gel cassette to prevent leaks.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Separated proteins within the gel, visible by the protein ladder.
troubleshooting: If bands are smeared: Reduce voltage or ensure buffer is fresh.
If no separation: Check power supply and buffer levels.

steps.title: Transfer Proteins to PVDF Membrane
steps.body_md: Transfer the separated proteins from the SDS-PAGE gel to a PVDF membrane using a wet transfer system (e.g., Bio-Rad Mini Trans-Blot® Cell). Equilibrate the membrane in methanol for 30 seconds, then assemble the transfer sandwich. Transfer at 100 V for 60 minutes at 4°C with constant stirring.
steps.notes: Ensure no air bubbles are trapped between the gel and the membrane during sandwich assembly. PVDF membrane must be pre-wetted in methanol.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Proteins successfully transferred to the PVDF membrane.
troubleshooting: If transfer is incomplete: Increase transfer time or voltage, ensure buffer is fresh and cold.
If membrane shows white spots: Re-wet membrane thoroughly in methanol before assembly.

reagent_recipes.name: Transfer Buffer (Wester Blot)
reagent_recipes.notes: Do not adjust pH. Store at 4°C.

steps.title: Block Membrane
steps.body_md: Block the PVDF membrane in 5% non-fat dry milk (NFDM) dissolved in Tris-Buffered Saline with Tween® 20 (TBST) for 1 hour at room temperature with gentle shaking.
steps.notes: Blocking prevents non-specific antibody binding.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Membrane uniformly coated with blocking solution.
troubleshooting: If high background is observed: Increase blocking time or concentration of NFDM.

reagent_recipes.name: TBST (Tris-Buffered Saline with Tween® 20)
reagent_recipes.notes: Store at room temperature. Adjust pH to 7.5.

steps.title: Incubate with Primary Antibodies
steps.body_md: Incubate the membrane overnight at 4°C with the primary antibodies diluted in 5% BSA in TBST. Use the following dilutions: anti-claudin-1 (1:1000), anti-occludin (1:1000), and anti-GAPDH or anti-beta-actin (1:5000) as a loading control.
steps.notes: Use separate containers or strip and re-probe membrane if using multiple primary antibodies with similar molecular weights.
troubleshooting: If no signal: Check antibody dilutions, ensure primary antibody was added.
If high background: Increase blocking time or consider higher purity BSA.

steps.title: Wash Membrane
steps.body_md: Wash the membrane three times for 10 minutes each with TBST at room temperature with gentle shaking.
steps.notes: Thorough washing crucial for reducing background noise.
troubleshooting: If high background persists: Increase wash duration or number of washes.

steps.title: Incubate with Secondary Antibodies
steps.body_md: Incubate the membrane with horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-conjugated secondary antibodies diluted 1:5000 in 5% NFDM in TBST for 1 hour at room temperature with gentle shaking.
reagents_referenced: HRP-conjugated secondary antibody (anti-rabbit or anti-mouse, depending on primary host)
steps.notes: Protect secondary antibody solution from strong light.
troubleshooting: If high background on control lanes: Consider reducing secondary antibody concentration.

steps.title: Final Washes
steps.body_md: Wash the membrane four times for 10 minutes each with TBST at room temperature with gentle shaking.
steps.notes: These washes are critical for removing unbound secondary antibody and reducing background noise.
troubleshooting: If background remains high: Increase wash duration or number of washes.

steps.title: Detect Chemiluminescence
steps.body_md: Incubate the membrane with enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) substrate for 1-5 minutes, following the manufacturer's recommendations. Immediately after incubation, capture images using a chemiluminescence imaging system (e.g., Bio-Rad ChemiDoc MP Imaging System). Adjust exposure time for optimal signal without saturation.
steps.notes: Work quickly to prevent substrate exhaustion and ensure consistent signal across samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Visible protein bands corresponding to claudin-1, occludin, and loading control.
troubleshooting: If signal is too weak: Increase exposure time or ensure the ECL reagents are fresh.
If signal is saturated: Reduce exposure time or dilute samples more for future experiments.

steps.title: Quantify Band Intensity
steps.body_md: Quantify the band intensity using image analysis software (e.g., Image Lab software, Bio-Rad). Normalize the intensity of claudin-1 and occludin bands to the intensity of the GAPDH or beta-actin loading control band for each sample.
steps.notes: Ensure consistent region of interest (ROI) selection for all bands during quantification.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Normalized relative protein expression values for claudin-1 and occludin for all samples.
